name: Japanese Creation Myths
shortDescription: The Japanese creation myths describe the genesis of the world, the emergence of the first deities, and the creation of the Japanese archipelago and its people.
description: The Japanese creation myths, primarily found in the Kojiki (Record of Ancient Matters) and the Nihon Shoki (Chronicles of Japan), detail the formation of the cosmos and the birth of the first deities (kami). Initially, the universe was a formless void, a chaotic mass resembling an egg. From this chaos, Takamagahara, the High Plain of Heaven, emerged, and the first three kami spontaneously appeared: Amenominakanushi, Takamimusubi, and Kamimusubi. These deities were solitary and soon concealed themselves. Subsequently, other generations of kami appeared, eventually leading to Izanagi and Izanami, the divine couple who were tasked with solidifying the floating land. They used the Amenonuhoko (Heavenly Jeweled Spear) to stir the primordial ocean, and when they lifted the spear, drops of salty water solidified into Onogoro Island. They descended to this island and built a palace called Yahiro-dono. They then performed a ritual to consummate their union, resulting in the birth of various islands that form the Japanese archipelago, as well as numerous other kami. Izanami died giving birth to Kagutsuchi (fire deity), and Izanagi, grief-stricken, followed her to Yomi (the underworld). He failed to retrieve her, and upon his return, he performed purification rituals, from which Amaterasu (sun goddess), Tsukuyomi (moon god), and Susanoo (storm god) were born. These three became the most important deities in the Japanese pantheon, with Amaterasu becoming the ancestress of the Japanese Imperial Family. The myths are crucial for understanding Japanese cosmology, the divine origins of the nation, and the relationship between humans and the kami. The myths also serve as a foundation for Shinto beliefs and practices, emphasizing purity, harmony with nature, and reverence for ancestors.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.author: Ō no Yasumaro (translator: Basil Hall Chamberlain)

sources.title: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.author: Various (translator: W.G. Aston)
